sections.heading: S
sections.section_value: The patient reports persistent right-sided abdominal pain, described as a squeezing, severe pain that comes and goes, rated as 8/10. He experiences nausea and vomiting with the pain. The pain is temporally linked to eating, specifically occurring shortly after or two hours after meals. He enjoys eating and has recently adopted a keto diet, losing 25 pounds over three months. He reports no fever or vomiting. He has a family history of gallbladder disease, with both parents having had their gallbladders removed. He denies blood in stool.

sections.heading: O
sections.section_value: Blood pressure is 128/88. Respiratory rate is 16. Pulse rate is 68. Oxygen saturation is 99% on room air. Auscultation reveals regular, rate, and rhythm heart sounds. Bowel sounds are present and soft. Abdomen is soft and nondistended. Slight guarding is noted in the right upper quadrant. Murphy's signs are present. The ultrasound reveals multiple gallstones in the gallbladder and a common bile duct size of 4.1 mm. The clinician performed a physical exam revealing no fever, no ectopic beats, no clicks, rubs, or murmurs. Vital signs are within normal limits.

sections.heading: A
sections.section_value: The patient is diagnosed with mild cholecystitis with gallstones. The clinician recommends a laparoscopic cholecystectomy to remove the gallbladder.

sections.heading: P
sections.section_value: The clinician recommends a laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The patient will spend the night in the hospital post-procedure for monitoring. The patient should avoid high-fat foods after surgery. The patient can expect to be back on their feet within 24 hours and can walk around within a day or two. The clinician will arrange a follow-up appointment in approximately one week. The nurse will obtain paperwork for diabetes management. The clinician will schedule a follow-up with the patient's endocrinologist.
